options.outcome: It offers no resistance — you pass through cold air and a brief cascade of half-remembered smells: a specific kitchen, a specific rain. You do not look back. The shade, if it watches, says nothing.

name: Alchemist Mourne
text: An alchemist has set up a field laboratory in what was once a guardroom, surrounded by equipment that should not survive this far down. Her goggles glow faintly green. "Oh, a live one. Excellent. I've been working on something that needs a test subject, and before you refuse — I'm offering fair compensation. I'm an alchemist, not a monster."

options.label: Volunteer as test subject (10 HP)
options.outcome: The compound tastes of copper and overripe plums. There is a moment of alarming internal heat, then it passes. "Excellent. Tissue response nominal. Better than nominal, actually." She scribbles notes while your body begins to quietly repair itself, wounds knitting at a rate you find both reassuring and slightly uncanny.

options.label: Buy her regeneration salve (200 gold)
options.outcome: "Tested and approved," she says, by which she means tested on herself, which you realize when she pulls up her sleeve to show old burn scars in various stages of alchemical healing. The salve is thick and smells terrible and works immediately.

options.label: "Hard pass."
options.outcome: "Noted," she says, without offense. "Healthy skepticism of unsolicited chemistry. Good instinct, actually. Come back if you change your mind — I'll be here until I run out of subjects or the dungeon runs out of patience with me." She returns to her bubbling apparatus.

name: The Oracle of Endings
text: She is seated on a throne of cracked stone that was clearly not built for her, surrounded by candles whose flames burn straight down instead of up. Her eyes are open and entirely white. "I have been waiting," she says, before you have made a sound. "Not for you specifically. For anyone with enough momentum to reach this depth. Sit. Or don't. I will speak regardless."

options.label: Offer mana for a true vision (15 MP)
options.outcome: She takes your offered mana by pressing two fingers to your temple and drawing it out like smoke from a jar. What she gives in return cannot be described — not a vision exactly, more a restructuring, a knowing of things at angles you did not have before. When it ends, she withdraws her fingers. "You are wiser than you were. It will feel that way too, eventually."

options.label: Ask her to bless your journey
options.outcome: She considers the gold for a long time. "This is not what oracles are for," she says, and pockets it anyway. The blessing she places on you is quiet and thorough, like good weather — you will not notice it until you should have died and didn't.

options.label: "I will make my own ending."
options.outcome: Her white eyes find you with an accuracy that makes the claim feel examined. "Yes," she says. "That is one of the options." She folds her hands and says nothing more, and after a while you realize you have been dismissed.

name: Sir Lorvaine the Certain
text: A knight in immaculate but deeply outdated armor stands squarely in your path. "I am Sir Lorvaine the Certain," he announces, "and I have a riddle. If you answer correctly, I will share with you the gifts of my failed quest. If you answer incorrectly, I will... also share them. The riddle is mostly a formality at this point. I'm very lonely."

options.label: Hear his riddle
options.outcome: The riddle is genuinely difficult and takes the better part of an hour. Sir Lorvaine interrupts several times to offer hints, then apologizes for the hints, then offers more hints. Eventually he declares you correct regardless of your answer and presents you with an accessory from his saddlebag with enormous ceremony.

options.label: Skip the riddle; offer gold (175 gold)
options.outcome: "Ah. A pragmatist." He seems genuinely relieved. "I was rather losing confidence in the riddle by iteration forty-seven anyway." He exchanges the gold for an accessory with the crisp efficiency of someone who has been waiting to do normal commerce for years.

options.label: Wish him well and continue
options.outcome: "Of course, of course," he says gamely, stepping aside. "The quest does not pause for company. I understand. Entirely. Carry on." His cheerfulness is very slightly too loud. You hear him composing a new riddle as you walk away.

name: The Forgotten Fragment
text: The light ahead is not coming from a torch. It is coming from something that is trying very hard to look like a person and almost succeeding. "I apologize for the luminosity," it says. "I am what remains of a deity that was largely forgotten. I have a great deal of residual power and nowhere to put it. Would you accept a gift? I find the giving helps."

options.label: Accept the gift with open hands
options.outcome: It steps forward and cups your face briefly with hands that are warm in the way that sunlight on stone is warm — secondhand, patient. Something wraps around you: not armor, exactly, but the memory of protection, invested in your skin. "That was a good fragment," it says, dimmer now. "I feel better. Thank you."

options.label: Ask its name
options.outcome: It pauses for a long time. "I don't remember," it says at last, without apparent distress. "I remember what I was for — hope, I think, or the specific kind of hope that survives bad odds. That feels right." It brightens slightly with the admission, then dims, and you are alone in the corridor.

options.label: Decline politely
options.outcome: "That is perfectly fine," it says, with the practiced grace of a being that has been rejected on a theological level and survived it. "Safe passage, then. Or as safe as it gets from here." It dims itself courteously as you pass, like a lamp turned low.

name: Pellok the Very Lost
text: A merchant in a wine merchant's apron is sitting on a crate labeled FINE VINTAGES, looking at a map upside-down with tremendous concentration. He does not hear you until you are quite close. "GAH. Oh. Sorry. Sorry. Do you — is this the 40th floor? I was told there was a marketplace on the 40th floor. I am very much getting the sense it was not on the 40th floor."

options.label: Buy a bottle from him (100 gold)
options.outcome: He is so relieved to make a sale that he nearly hugs you. The wine is, inexplicably, excellent — a deep red that tastes of a summer several decades ago and warms you to the marrow. Your wounds respond favorably to the vintage. He marks you on his route map as a 'reliable customer.'

options.label: Give him food to help him back up
options.outcome: He accepts the food with enormous gratitude. "You are a saint. A genuine saint. Here — take this, I've been using it as a paperweight. The previous owner said it was powerful. They were very confident about it." He presses a potion into your hands and begins the long walk upward, muttering.

options.label: Point him vaguely upward
options.outcome: "Up," he repeats, as though this had not occurred to him. "Yes. That — that tracks. Thank you. Tremendous. Right." He hoists his crate, takes two steps in the wrong direction, corrects himself, and begins a very long journey home.

name: The Stone Warden's Voice
text: The wall speaks. Not through a crack or a carving — the whole wall, slowly, grinding syllables out of compression and age. "I HAVE BEEN IN THIS WALL," it says, "FOR ELEVEN THOUSAND YEARS. I HAVE WATCHED MANY PASS. I HAVE SOMETHING TO OFFER. IF YOU WILL LISTEN FIRST."

options.label: Listen to the wall's story (30 SP)
options.outcome: It takes a long time. Eleven thousand years compressed into an hour of stone-slow speech — civilizations that used this corridor as a parade route, as a grave, as a shortcut, as a place of worship. You ache from standing still so long, but when it finishes, it breathes out a warmth through the rock that settles on you like a blessing earned and given freely. "THANK YOU," it rumbles. "GO WELL."

options.label: Press your palm to the stone (15 MP)
options.outcome: The stone is warmer than it should be. Something flows in the opposite direction of the cold — a mineral patience, a geological steadiness, the endurance of things that outlast every emergency by simply refusing to end. Your body absorbs it as stamina. The wall sighs in gratitude.

options.label: Walk past in respectful silence
options.outcome: "YOU ARE BUSY," the wall says, without judgment. "I UNDERSTAND. THE LIVING ALWAYS ARE." A pause, ground out in slow syllables. "IT IS ONE OF THE THINGS I MISS."

name: (Something Watching)
text: Nothing is there. And yet: the sound of a quill scratching on parchment. A throat cleared politely. The faint impression of a chair being occupied. "I catalog the deeds of those who descend," says a voice at desk-height, precisely where no desk is. "I have questions, if you have answers. And I pay for my interviews."

options.label: Agree to be interviewed
options.outcome: The interview is thorough. It asks about your earliest memory, your greatest fear, your theory on what lies at the dungeon's bottom, and whether you prefer mornings. It transcribes your answers with the focused energy of someone who finds all answers equally important. When it finishes, a purse of gold appears on the floor in front of you. "Excellent data," it says warmly. "Truly."

options.label: Ask who it archives for
options.outcome: A long pause. The quill stops. "That is the question," it says, and you hear something that might be a smile in the voice. "Isn't it. The honest answer is: I archive for myself. I have been doing this long enough that 'for whom' stopped mattering and 'that it is done' took its place." The quill resumes.

options.label: Decline the interview
options.outcome: "Noted," says the voice, and you hear your refusal being noted. "That is, itself, a data point. Walk safe." The scratching of the quill follows you down the corridor for longer than seems possible, then stops all at once.

name: Dying Hero
text: A towering warrior slumps against the wall, his golden armor shattered, a dozen wounds weeping light rather than blood. "I made it to the ninety-second floor once," he rasps, "before the thing with a thousand mouths found me. I won't make it further. But you — you still have breath in you."

options.label: Give him a healing potion
options.outcome: He shakes his head when you offer coin, but you press it into his hands anyway. He looks at it for a long moment, then presses his sword hilt to yours. "Take the edge from Berengar's blade. Let it taste whatever lies below." Your weapon gleams with new purpose.

options.label: Sit with him a while
options.outcome: You say nothing. He says nothing. The silence between you is the companionship of people who understand what this place costs. When he finally stills, you feel something settle over you — a dying man's final wish for another's survival. You feel blessed.

options.label: Keep moving — there is no time
options.outcome: He doesn't blame you. He just watches you go with eyes that have seen too many things walk past. You hear him whisper something as you round the corner — a name, perhaps, or a prayer.

name: Grey-Eyed Woman
text: A woman stands in the corridor as though the dungeon was built around her. Her eyes are the silver-grey of storm clouds before lightning, and an owl sits on her shoulder that is not quite an owl. "You are further than most," she says. "I am curious whether your mind has sharpened to match your ambition."

options.label: Offer food as tribute to her
options.outcome: She takes the offering with an unhurried grace and sets it before the owl, who regards it with solemn intelligence. "The gift of sight is not given to those who look — only to those who truly see." The world sharpens around you, edges crisper, shadows less secret. Your Perception increases.

options.label: "What lies at the bottom?"
options.outcome: She tilts her head. "A question. Good." The owl blinks slowly. "At the bottom lies the thing that asked itself what lies above. Everything looks up, child. Even the abyss has ambitions." She steps aside, and the corridor behind her seems somehow shorter.

options.label: Bow respectfully and pass
options.outcome: She inclines her head in return, a gesture that carries more weight than any blessing you can name. The owl watches you until you are out of sight. You suspect it watches you still.

name: The Enumerated Self
text: It is shaped like a person the way a drawing is shaped like a person — the right number of limbs, in roughly the right places, but flat in some dimension you cannot name. It speaks without a mouth: "We have been cataloguing experiences. We have fourteen thousand, nine hundred and two. You have more. We would like some of yours."

options.label: Trade a memory for its knowledge
options.outcome: You feel something leave you — not pain, but absence. A room from your childhood you can no longer quite picture. The Enumerated Self shudders with something that might be pleasure. In return, it presses fourteen thousand, nine hundred and three small certainties into your mind. The dungeon becomes briefly, brilliantly legible.

options.label: "What number are you on now?"
options.outcome: "Fourteen thousand, nine hundred and three," it says, and you realize it is looking at you. "The experience of being asked a question by someone who is not afraid of the answer." It folds itself into a smaller space and then is simply not there. You feel vaguely counted.

options.label: Back away slowly
options.outcome: It watches you retreat with an expression that has been assembled from references rather than felt. "We will catalogue this also," it says. "The experience of watching something leave." Somehow, that is the worst thing it could have said.

name: Ortolan the Last Merchant
text: A stout man in a coat with too many pockets sits on a chest that radiates unnatural warmth, counting coins that seem to disappear between his fingers before being counted again. "Ortolan's Emporium — deepest shop in the world, est. longer than you've been alive!" he announces cheerfully. "What'll it be?"

options.label: Buy something from his stock
options.outcome: He rummages through the chest with alarming depth — his arm disappears to the shoulder before he produces something with a triumphant grunt. "Finest stock this side of the abyss! Probably. I stopped checking labels after level sixty."

options.label: Buy a scroll from him
options.outcome: He produces a scroll with a flourish, sealed with wax in the shape of a coin. "Knowledge, the only currency that appreciates!" he says. "That's something a customer told me. I mostly just sell things."

options.label: "How do you survive down here?"
options.outcome: He taps the side of his nose. "Good prices and fast feet, friend. The monsters leave me alone because I give them favorable terms on bulk purchases." He winks. You cannot tell if he is joking. You strongly suspect he is not joking.

name: The Speaking Absence
text: There is a place in the corridor where the stones simply stop — not a wall, not a door, but an ending. From within the ending, something addresses you in a voice that arrives already understood: "You have walked a long way to reach the edge of what is written. We did not expect a visitor."

options.label: Step closer and speak with it
options.outcome: The closer you get, the more you feel yourself becoming slightly optional — as though the universe is reminding itself why you should exist. But the Absence speaks warmly, and when you step back, you carry something of its regard with you. The world holds you a little more carefully than before.

options.label: Ask what lies beyond the ending
options.outcome: A long silence that contains multitudes. Then: "More of what you would call 'nothing,' and less of what you would call 'something,' until the two become indistinguishable and the distinction stops mattering." A pause. "It is actually very peaceful. You should visit. Not yet, though."

options.label: Look away and walk past
options.outcome: Behind you, the Absence says nothing. This is somehow more unsettling than if it had called after you. You resist the urge to look back. You are not entirely sure you succeed.

name: Spectral Archivist
text: A translucent woman in scholar's robes moves along the corridor running her fingers across stones as though reading text written in the rock itself. She turns when she hears you, and her eyes are full of tiny writing. "Oh — a living one. I haven't seen one of those in what must be centuries. Tell me, do the libraries of the surface still stand?"

options.label: Give her a scroll from your pack
options.outcome: She takes the scroll with trembling, half-solid hands and presses it to her chest. The writing in her eyes shifts and reforms. "I have not had new text in so long," she whispers. "Let me give you something in return — I know every stone of these depths by now." The dungeon opens itself to your mind's eye.

options.label: Tell her about the surface world
options.outcome: You talk for longer than you intended — about markets and weather and the smell of rain on cobblestones. She listens with total, heartbreaking attention. When you finish, she stands straighter. "Thank you. I had nearly forgotten what the world was for." You feel the warmth of the exchange settle into your bones.

options.label: "I cannot stay. I am sorry."
options.outcome: "Of course. Everyone is always going somewhere down here." She turns back to her stones and resumes her endless reading. You feel the specific weight of an apology that was both necessary and insufficient.

name: The General Out of Time
text: A man in ancient armor stands at perfect attention, studying a battle map drawn on the floor in chalk that does not smudge. His army, you realize, is indicated by a single mark. He looks up at you with the bright, slightly too-focused eyes of someone who has been waiting a very long time. "Soldier! Report. What is the current strategic situation?"

options.label: Play along and give him a "report"
options.outcome: You invent a battle. He listens with furious, grateful attention, making marks on his map, nodding at your fabrications as though they confirm long-held suspicions. "Good. Then the third flank must move." He grips your arm with a general's certainty. "Double-time, soldier. I'll buy you a moment." You feel your feet lighten.

options.label: Tell him the truth gently
options.outcome: He listens. His face does the complicated thing faces do when time catches up with them all at once. Then he straightens his shoulders. "I see. Then I have been standing guard over nothing." A pause. "No. Not nothing. I have been standing." He presses his rations into your hands. "Take these. Your war is not yet over."

options.label: Salute and move on
options.outcome: He returns the salute with crisp, unbroken discipline. "At ease, soldier." You walk past his chalk army and his chalk enemy and his single chalk mark, and you do not look back, because some things deserve the dignity of an unwitnessed vigil.

name: Fast-Talking Stranger
text: A young man in traveling clothes that somehow look freshly pressed despite everything leans against a pillar, spinning a coin between his fingers. He's grinning before you even reach him, as though your arrival is the punchline to a joke he's been sitting on for hours. "Incredible," he says. "You actually made it this far. I owe the Fates an apology."

options.label: Trade gold for something valuable
options.outcome: He catches the coin with a speed that shouldn't be possible and produces something from behind your ear — literally, behind your ear — with the air of someone who finds this less impressive than you do. "Special delivery," he says. "I was going to keep it, but you have a better face for it."

options.label: Ask him to speed your steps
options.outcome: He looks almost offended that you'd pay for something so basic, but pockets the gold with practiced ease. He snaps his fingers near your heels and something electric runs up your spine. "Try not to run into any walls," he advises, and is already somewhere else before you can respond.

options.label: "What are you really?"
options.outcome: He spreads his hands, the picture of innocence. "A traveler! A merchant! A concerned passerby!" He ticks them off on his fingers. "Look, the real question is what are YOU really, and I think we both know the answer to that is a lot more interesting." He flips the coin at you. When you catch it, he's gone. The coin is warm.

name: The Final Philosopher
text: An ancient man sits cross-legged on the bare stone, eyes closed, head bowed in thought. He has no possessions. He has no food. He seems to have no need of either. When you approach, he opens eyes that contain the specific exhaustion of someone who has thought about everything and found it wanting. "Ah," he says. "The question with legs."

options.label: Ask him the nature of this place
options.outcome: He speaks for a long time. His words are simple — a child could understand each one — but arranged in an order that does something permanent to the architecture of your thinking. When he finishes, the dungeon looks the same but means something different. Your Wisdom increases.

options.label: Sit with him in silence
options.outcome: You sit. He sits. The dungeon makes its dungeon noises at a respectful distance. After a time that could be minutes or hours, he says: "Good. Most people who need to think forget to stop moving first." You feel your mind clarify like water that has been allowed to settle.

options.label: "I don't have time to philosophize."
options.outcome: He nods slowly. "That is itself a philosophy," he says, without judgment or irony. "And it has brought you this far. Perhaps it will carry you further still." He closes his eyes again. You feel, unexpectedly, that you have his blessing.

name: Weeping Prophet
text: A woman kneels in the center of the corridor, tears streaming freely, though her expression is serene rather than anguished — the face of someone who has made peace with being ignored. "I know what you will face," she says. "I know what you will choose. I have said so many times, to so many people, and none of them believed me until it was done."

options.label: "Tell me anyway. I will listen."
options.outcome: She looks at you with an expression that is the entire history of being taken seriously for the first time. She speaks quickly, like someone who knows the window might close. The words wash over you and sink in, and for a time the dungeon holds no secrets from you.

options.label: Offer her something to restore her strength
options.outcome: She takes the food with both hands and eats with the careful gratitude of someone who has forgotten how it tastes. "You fed a prophet," she says softly. "The old texts say something about that. Something good." She touches your cheek, and you feel the world tilt slightly in your favor.

options.label: "Prophecy is a burden I cannot share."
options.outcome: "Yes," she says simply. "I know." She watches you go with the patient, ruined dignity of someone who has been correct about everything and helped no one. The sound of her weeping does not follow you. You almost wish it did.

name: Dim Light in the Stone
text: A crack in the wall pulses with warm golden light, too rhythmic to be fire, too alive to be stone. When you lean close, you feel the heat of something enormous pressed into a space far too small for it. A voice comes from within the crack: "Smaller than I was. But not gone. Traveler — I can offer you something, if you offer something in return."

options.label: Offer your blood to the light
options.outcome: The blood touches the crack and the light surges, briefly, brilliantly. "There," says the voice, with something like relief. "That was nourishing. I had nearly forgotten what living things feel like." A warmth travels up your arm and spreads, and you feel your body begin to quietly, persistently knit itself together.

options.label: Offer your mana to the trapped god
options.outcome: The magic leaves you in a slow, warm exhale, drawn into the stone like breath on a cold day. "Ah. Old power. Thank you." A long pause, then: "I am too diminished to repay you as I once could. But take this — the least I have to offer." The warmth returns, settling into your wounds, sealing them.

options.label: Press on without engaging it
options.outcome: The light dims as you pass, like eyes closing. "Another time, perhaps," says the voice, with a patience that has been accumulating for longer than civilizations. You feel the light watching you long after the crack is out of sight.

name: Abyss Cartographer
text: A gaunt woman covered in ink stains crouches over an enormous map spread on the floor, adding detail with a pen that never seems to run dry. The map, you realize, depicts a dungeon that is subtly but definitely wrong in ways you cannot quite specify. She looks up with brilliant, slightly manic eyes. "You! You've come from above! TELL me what you saw up there!"

options.label: Trade your observations for her maps
options.outcome: You spend an exhausting quarter-hour answering increasingly specific questions about corridor widths and stone textures and the exact smell of level eighty-three. She records everything with furious precision. "WONDERFUL. Here —" She tears off a section of map and presses it to your forehead. The dungeon's layout floods your mind.

options.label: Buy a wand from her equipment
options.outcome: "Oh, that old thing," she says, waving at what you'd assumed was just a measuring instrument. "I use it to check for hollow walls. You can have it — I've got three more and they keep pointing at each other, which is philosophically troubling." The gold vanishes into a coat with improbable pocket depth.

options.label: Wish her luck and continue down
options.outcome: "Luck!" she repeats with the joy of someone who's been given a gift they didn't know they wanted. "What a concept. I've been working on statistical modeling but 'luck' is so much more portable." She's already back to her map. You're fairly sure you will appear on it.

name: Disreputable Imp
text: A small, red, deeply self-satisfied creature sits atop a crate with 'DEFINITELY FINE GOODS' stenciled on the side in handwriting that changes between letters. It points at you with a claw the size of a needle and announces: "YOU. You look like someone with gold and poor decision-making skills. Perfect. PERFECT. I have things."

options.label: Buy something from his definitely-fine crate
options.outcome: He produces something from the crate with theatrical effort, requiring both arms and one foot. "Finest quality! Mostly! The cursed ones are in the OTHER crate and I DEFINITELY didn't mix them up this time." He takes your gold and bites it experimentally. "Acceptable."

options.label: Ask what's actually in the crate
options.outcome: He stares at you. Then at the crate. Then back at you. "...Conceptually? Hope." He pauses. "Physically? I'm not entirely sure anymore. Something shifted on level ninety and I think the inventory reorganized itself on philosophical grounds." He looks genuinely concerned for a moment. Then the moment passes.

options.label: "No thank you, actually."
options.outcome: His face falls with operatic despair. "NO. No no no. Do you know how few people get to level eighty-something? Do you know how long I've been waiting?" He watches you go. "I'll be here!" he calls. "I am always here. I don't know how to leave!"

name: Fallen Constellation
text: In a chamber with no ceiling — or rather, a ceiling that has been replaced by deep black space — a figure made entirely of connected stars wheels slowly in the middle of the air. It is beautiful in the way that things are beautiful when they are not meant for you. It notices you and descends. "We fell," it says simply. "We have been falling for some time."

options.label: Ask it to show you the sky it came from
options.outcome: It reaches out with a hand made of light and distance and touches your forehead. For a moment you are not in the dungeon. You are somewhere enormous and cold and breathtaking, and you understand why something might spend eternity simply being part of such a sky. You return carrying a small piece of that regard.

options.label: Offer to light a path for it with a torch
options.outcome: It looks at your small flame with an expression so gentle it nearly breaks something in your chest. "You would light the way for us," it says softly, "when we are made of light." But it follows your torch for a few steps anyway, and the warmth it shares with you in return is vast and generous.

options.label: Stand still and watch it for a moment
options.outcome: You watch. It wheels. The stars that make up its shape pulse slowly, and you think you can almost read them — a story written in fire across impossible distances. When you finally move on, it watches you go, and somehow the dungeon is a little less dark for a while.

name: The One Behind You
text: You see it first in a shard of reflective stone — a figure standing just behind you, mimicking your posture exactly. When you turn, nothing is there. When you look back at the stone, it is not mimicking you anymore. It is watching you, with its head tilted slightly, like a student who has a question. It raises one hand. It waits.

options.label: Raise your hand back
options.outcome: You raise your hand. It nods, slowly, with visible satisfaction. Then it steps through the mirror and past you — you feel it pass like cold water — and it vanishes into the dungeon ahead of you. Where it walked, nothing notices you for a long while. It has taught the darkness your outline, and the darkness is looking elsewhere.

options.label: Break the reflective stone
options.outcome: The stone shatters and the figure in the reflection shatters with it — not violently, but the way an expression falls from a face when it thinks no one is watching. You breathe hard. The effort cost you. But there is something releasing in the violence of it, and the tension leaves your body along with the shards.

options.label: Walk past without acknowledging it
options.outcome: You feel it follow you with its gaze across every reflective surface in the corridor. Wet stone. A dropped shield. The dark surface of still water in a crack. You do not look at any of them. You are fairly confident it respects this. You are less confident it stops following.

name: The Last Champion
text: A massive warrior sits polishing armor that no longer has a purpose to be polished for. He does it anyway, with the methodical attention of someone who has run out of things to do but not out of discipline. He looks up at you and something wakes behind his eyes. "Still coming down, are they? Good. The deep floors need witnesses."

options.label: Ask him to train you for a while
options.outcome: He trains you hard and without mercy, correcting your grip and your footwork and the angle of your shoulders with the blunt precision of a man who was taught by people who learned the hard way. An hour passes like a grinding stone. When he's done, he taps your weapon. "There. It knows how to be held properly now."

options.label: Share a meal with him
options.outcome: He receives the food with the dignity of a man who has forgotten what it means to receive anything. He eats slowly. "My first meal shared in..." He counts. Stops counting. "Thank you." He refills something in your pack — rations you didn't know you were missing. "You'll need it more than I do."

options.label: "What are you still guarding?"
options.outcome: He looks down at the armor in his hands. The question takes a long time to reach an answer. "The idea," he finally says, "that someone should still be here when the next one comes through. So they know the road has been walked before." He gestures you onward. "It has been walked before."

name: Medical Construct
text: A golem made of white stone stands in an alcove, its chest engraved with a serpent-and-staff symbol worn nearly smooth by centuries. It turns toward you with the careful movements of something built to handle fragile things. "SUBJECT ASSESSED: ALIVE. CONDITION: SUBOPTIMAL. SERVICES AVAILABLE. INDICATE REQUIREMENT."

options.label: Request a full physical restoration
options.outcome: It works with the impersonal efficiency of something that has been doing this for seven hundred years and finds it neither interesting nor tedious — it simply finds it correct. When it finishes, your wounds are sealed, your breathing is even, and it has left a small receipt in your pocket in a language that may not exist anymore.

options.label: Request stamina treatment
options.outcome: It administers something it calls a "musculoskeletal optimization procedure," which involves exactly three minutes of targeted pressure to specific points that you didn't know you had. You feel the deep exhaustion of eighty-some floors drain out of you like water from a wrung cloth.

options.label: "I am fine. Thank you."
options.outcome: It processes this for a moment. "ASSESSMENT INDICATES STATEMENT INACCURATE. HOWEVER: PATIENT AUTONOMY PROTOCOLS APPLY." It steps back into its alcove. "SAFE TRAVELS, SUBJECT." It says this with something that sounds, against all probability, like it means it.

name: The Nameless
text: It does not have a shape so much as it has an implication of one — a suggestion that something is there, organized in a way that gestures toward personhood. When it speaks, the words are felt rather than heard: "You have a name. We have observed this. Names are a form of anchor. We have been drifting for some time. We would offer a trade."

options.label: Teach it your name's meaning
options.outcome: You explain your name — where it came from, what it means, the weight of it. The Nameless receives this with something that might be reverence. You feel it try the name on, tentatively, like a coat it isn't sure fits. In return, it wraps something of its own boundlessness around you — a protection that comes from having nothing left to lose.

options.label: Give it a name freely, ask nothing
options.outcome: You give it a name without price. It holds very still for a long moment — the stillness of something that has never been held before. "We did not expect this," it admits. And then it blesses you, clumsily, earnestly, with the overflowing gratitude of a being who has just discovered what it means to be given something for nothing.

options.label: Decline to share your name
options.outcome: "Understood," it says, without disappointment — or perhaps with a disappointment so vast it has become indistinguishable from acceptance. "Names are powerful. You are wise to protect yours." It drifts aside to let you pass. You feel it watching the space where you were long after you have gone.

name: Keeper of Last Memories
text: A robed figure sits surrounded by softly glowing motes — hundreds of them, drifting like embers from a dying fire, each one pulsing with its own quiet warmth. "Each of these," it says without turning, "is the last thought of someone who did not make it back. I keep them so they are not simply lost. You may carry one, if you choose."

options.label: Accept a fallen adventurer's last memory
options.outcome: The mote settles on your palm and dissolves. For a moment you are someone else — someone who died on level ninety-four, who knew exactly where the shadow-thing waited, who almost made it. The knowledge they died for is yours now. You feel the weight and the gift of it equally.

options.label: Leave a memory of the surface world here
options.outcome: You concentrate on something bright — a morning, a meal, a face — and it lifts gently away and joins the other motes. The Keeper watches it go. "Thank you. The collection is heavy with endings. It is good to add something that felt like a beginning." The loss settles into you strangely, like a wound that has been given proper care.

options.label: Observe the motes in silence and move on
options.outcome: You stand for a moment among the last lights of the lost. The Keeper does not press you. The motes drift around you without touching, as though they sense you are still using yourself. When you go, you feel them watching you with something that is almost hope.
